content.text: What Are Developer Docs and Runbooks in an IDP

Developer documentation and runbooks in an Internal Developer Portal (IDP) are the authoritative, searchable, and freshness-tracked body of knowledge that agent developers and operations engineers use to understand, develop, and operate agentic AI systems. Developer docs cover the 'how to build' dimension: API references for platform services, getting-started guides for agent templates, configuration schemas, golden path walkthroughs, and integration tutorials. Runbooks cover the 'how to operate' dimension: step-by-step response procedures for specific alerts, troubleshooting guides for known failure modes, rollback instructions, and escalation paths.

The IDP portal is the integration point that makes documentation discoverable and maintainable. Instead of documentation scattered across Confluence, Notion, service GitHub repositories, Slack pinned messages, and tribal knowledge, the portal aggregates documentation from canonical sources, surfaces it through unified search, tracks freshness, and provides feedback mechanisms. For agent systems where the operational surface area includes LLM providers, eval infrastructure, retrieval systems, and tool integrations—each with distinct failure modes and operational procedures—having a unified documentation hub is a practical necessity rather than a convenience.
Why Docs and Runbooks in an IDP Matter

Documentation decay is the dominant documentation problem in engineering organizations. Documentation is written at the time of system launch, reflects the system as designed not as currently operated, and becomes increasingly stale as the system evolves. In agent systems, this decay is accelerated because: prompts change frequently without triggering documentation updates, model API behaviors evolve across versions, and operational procedures discovered through production incidents are rarely written back into runbooks. The IDP portal addresses decay by tracking document freshness, alerting owners when documentation has not been updated within a defined period, and making staleness visible to readers.

Runbooks that cannot be found during an incident are as useless as runbooks that do not exist. When an alert fires at 02:00 and the on-call engineer navigates to the IDP portal, searches for the alert name, and finds the exact runbook with step-by-step diagnosis steps and a link to the escalation contact, mean time to resolve drops significantly. When the engineer must search Confluence, check Slack history, and call someone to find the procedure, minutes or hours are lost. Runbook discoverability—measured by 'time to find the relevant procedure from alert notification'—is as important as runbook quality.
Core Benefits
Unified search across all documentation sources reduces time to find relevant content from minutes to seconds.
Freshness tracking and stale alerts prevent silent documentation decay, keeping docs usable during incidents.
Alert-to-runbook linking ensures on-call engineers navigate directly to the correct procedure from an active alert.
Change notifications keep subscribing engineers informed when procedures they rely on are updated.
Feedback mechanisms (helpful/not helpful ratings, inline comments) surface documentation gaps before they cause incident delays.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Portal integration overhead: wiring documentation sources into the portal requires initial setup and ongoing maintenance of sync integrations.
Freshness tracking requires documentation ownership assignment; undocumented owners cause freshness alerts to go unacknowledged.
Portal search quality depends on documentation structure and metadata quality; poorly structured docs remain hard to find even in a portal.
Documentation debt: migrating existing docs from Confluence, Notion, or scattered wikis into a portal-indexed structure is a significant upfront investment.
Over-reliance on search: complex troubleshooting flows may require judgment that search cannot surface; runbooks supplement but cannot replace experienced oncall engineers.
How Docs and Runbooks in an IDP Work

The portal aggregates documentation from canonical sources using sync integrations. Service repositories contain README.md and docs/ directories that are synced to the portal's service entity catalog. A dedicated runbook repository (ops/runbooks/) contains all operational procedures in Markdown format, versioned with the same rigor as code. Architecture decision records (ADRs) are stored in a dedicated repository and synced to the ADR catalog in the portal. The portal indexes all synced content and provides unified search with filters by document type (docs, runbook, ADR), service, team, and recency.

Freshness tracking assigns an owner to each document and records the last-modified date. A freshness policy defines the maximum age for each document type: runbooks must be updated within 90 days, getting-started guides within 180 days, API reference documentation within 30 days of the corresponding service release. When a document exceeds its freshness threshold, the portal marks it as stale and sends a weekly reminder to the owner. Staleness is visible to readers—a stale runbook displays a banner: 'This runbook was last updated 94 days ago. The owner has been notified to review it.' Readers can still use the runbook but are warned to verify the steps are current.

Alert-to-runbook linking connects observability alerts to the procedures that respond to them. In the alert configuration (Prometheus alerting rules, Datadog monitors), add a `runbook_url` annotation that points to the IDP portal URL for the relevant runbook. When the alert fires in PagerDuty or OpsGenie, the runbook link appears in the incident notification. Engineers click directly to the correct procedure without search. Maintaining these links requires that runbook URLs remain stable—use stable slug-based URLs in the portal rather than auto-generated IDs that change when documents are reorganized.
Architecture

attrs.alt: IDP documentation architecture: service repos, runbook repo, and ADR repository auto-sync to the portal, providing unified search, freshness tracking, alert linking, and feedback mechanisms

content.text: When to Apply This Pattern

Introduce IDP documentation management when the team has more than five engineers, more than three agent services, and is beginning to experience the 'where is that documented?' pain in incidents and onboarding. For smaller teams with one or two services, a well-maintained GitHub wiki may be sufficient. The IDP documentation pattern pays off when the documentation surface area exceeds what a single person can maintain mental models of.

Prioritize alert-to-runbook linking immediately after enabling a new alert or on-call rotation. An alert that fires without a linked runbook creates oncall friction immediately; the linking cost is small and the benefit is immediate. Deprioritize documentation migration from legacy systems until the new portal structure is validated—migrating thousands of outdated Confluence pages into a new portal preserves the content but not the staleness problem.
How to Implement

# Runbook Template — ops/runbooks/eval-accuracy-regression.md
---
title: "Eval Accuracy Regression Alert"
owner: "@platform/ml-quality"
last_reviewed: "2025-06-01"
alert_name: "EvalAccuracyRegression"
severity: high
services: [customer-agent, billing-agent]
tags: [eval, accuracy, model-quality]
---

# Eval Accuracy Regression

## Alert Context
Fires when eval suite accuracy on the golden dataset drops more than 2% below 
baseline within the past 24 hours.

## Immediate Steps (< 5 min)

1. Check the eval dashboard: [Eval Dashboard Link]
2. Identify which eval categories are failing (billing, support, etc.)
3. Check for recent deployments to the affected agent service:
   `git log --since="24 hours ago" -- src/agents/`

## Diagnosis Steps (5-15 min)

4. If a recent prompt change: review the diff and identify changed prompt sections
5. Run eval suite locally on last-known-good commit:
   `python evals/run_evals.py --commit <sha> --dataset golden_v2.jsonl`
6. Compare failing cases to identify the regression pattern

## Resolution Paths

**Path A: Prompt regression** — Revert the prompt change (git revert <sha>), redeploy
**Path B: Model API change** — Pin to previous model version in config, escalate to @platform/ml-quality
**Path C: Dataset staleness** — If baseline was reset recently, verify with @platform/ml-quality

## Escalation
- Unresolved after 30 min: page @platform/ml-quality-lead
- Production accuracy < 88%: page @engineering-director

# Prometheus alerting rule with runbook_url annotation
groups:
  - name: agent-quality
    rules:
      - alert: EvalAccuracyRegression
        expr: |
          (eval_accuracy_current - eval_accuracy_baseline) / eval_accuracy_baseline < -0.02
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: high
          team: platform-ml-quality
        annotations:
          summary: "Eval accuracy has regressed more than 2% from baseline"
          description: "Current accuracy: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"
          runbook_url: "https://idp.example.com/runbooks/eval-accuracy-regression"
          dashboard_url: "https://grafana.example.com/d/eval-accuracy"
Worked Examples

Example 1 — Incident response with runbook link: At 03:15, an EvalAccuracyRegression alert fires and pages the on-call engineer. The PagerDuty notification includes a direct runbook link. The engineer clicks the link, follows the immediate steps, identifies a recent prompt change as the cause, reverts it in 8 minutes, and closes the alert by 03:28. Total incident duration: 13 minutes. Without the runbook link, the engineer would have spent 15-20 minutes searching for the relevant procedure.

Example 2 — Stale runbook caught by freshness tracking: The IDP freshness tracker identifies that the 'Rate Limit Recovery' runbook has not been updated in 97 days, exceeding the 90-day policy. The owner (@platform/sre) receives a weekly reminder. The SRE reviews the runbook, finds that the OpenAI rate limit error codes changed in a recent API update, updates the runbook with the correct error code ranges, and marks it reviewed. The update notification is sent to the 12 engineers who subscribed to the runbook, informing them of the change.

Example 3 — Onboarding accelerated by portal: A new engineer joins the platform team. Their first task is to understand the billing agent service. They open the IDP service catalog, find the billing-agent entry, and navigate to the linked documentation: architecture overview, API reference, getting-started guide, relevant runbooks, and the service's active ADRs. Within two hours they have a clear mental model of the service's structure, its failure modes, and its operational history. Previously this onboarding required a 2-hour walkthrough with a senior engineer.
Real-World Application

A B2B AI company running eight agent services implemented a Backstage-based IDP with documentation management after measuring that on-call engineers spent an average of 18 minutes searching for runbooks before beginning incident diagnosis. They migrated 42 runbooks from scattered Confluence pages to a structured ops/runbooks/ repository, added runbook_url annotations to all 28 active Prometheus alerts, and implemented freshness tracking with a 90-day policy. Six months later: time to find runbook during incidents dropped from 18 minutes to 2 minutes. The stale documentation rate (docs older than their freshness threshold) dropped from 58% to 12%. The engineering onboarding time for new hires dropped from 8 days (waiting for knowledge transfer from senior engineers) to 3 days.
Common Mistakes
Treating documentation migration as a big-bang project that must complete before the portal is useful, rather than incrementally migrating the most-used documents first.
Not assigning owners to documents, making freshness tracking alerts go unacknowledged.
Using auto-generated runbook URLs that break when documents are reorganized, breaking all alert annotations.
Linking alerts to runbooks but not maintaining the links when alerts are renamed or runbooks are merged.
Publishing documentation to the portal but not decommissioning the old Confluence or Notion sources, creating two competing sources of truth.
Best Practices
Use stable slug-based URLs for all runbooks (e.g., /runbooks/eval-accuracy-regression) and protect them with redirect rules when runbooks are renamed.
Include a 'last reviewed' date in every runbook front matter, separate from the git last-modified date, to distinguish meaningful reviews from typo fixes.
Add a 'verify in staging first' note to any runbook step that modifies production state, reducing the risk of on-call engineers causing additional incidents while responding.
Test runbooks quarterly in non-production incident drills: find an on-call engineer who did not write the runbook and have them follow it during a tabletop exercise.
Treat runbook quality as a definition-of-done criterion for new alert additions: no alert merges without a corresponding runbook in the IDP portal.
Performance and Scalability

Documentation portal search must be fast enough to be useful during incidents—a search that takes 10 seconds to return results will cause oncall engineers to fall back to Slack search or direct colleague contact. Implement full-text search with sub-second response time using Elasticsearch or Algolia with pre-indexed content. Index updates should be near-real-time (within 2 minutes of source document changes) rather than batched nightly, so updated runbooks are findable immediately after they are published.

As the documentation volume grows to thousands of documents across dozens of services, search result quality becomes critical. Implement relevance tuning: recently updated documents rank higher, documents with high 'helpful' ratings rank higher, documents matching the user's current team context rank higher than generic documents. Relevance tuning prevents search results from being dominated by old, high-word-count documents that keyword-match without being contextually relevant.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Comparison

Freshness Tracking
Alert Integration

Security and Reliability Considerations

Documentation and runbooks may contain sensitive operational information: escalation contact details, internal service URLs, configuration values, and details about known system weaknesses. Protect the IDP documentation portal with the same authentication and authorization controls as the rest of the engineering platform. Use role-based access control: runbooks for security-sensitive procedures (secret rotation, access revocation) should be visible only to engineers with the appropriate operational access. Do not publish internal documentation to public-facing portals or GitHub Pages.

The portal itself must be reliable: if it is unavailable during an incident, engineers cannot access runbooks. Host the portal on highly available infrastructure, separate from the systems it documents, so a production incident that takes down the agent services does not also take down the IDP. Consider a static site backup of critical runbooks that is hosted independently and accessible without portal authentication, providing a last-resort reference when the full IDP is unavailable.
Documentation Taxonomy for Agent Systems

Agent systems have a richer documentation taxonomy than conventional services because they involve multiple interacting components that each require different types of documentation. Define a clear taxonomy in the portal so developers know which document type to consult and which to create for new content. The taxonomy for agent systems typically includes: architecture overview (how the agent is structured and why key design decisions were made), prompt library (documented prompt templates with their intended use cases and known limitations), tool reference (documented tool definitions, input schemas, output schemas, and error conditions), eval documentation (golden dataset description, scoring methodology, and result interpretation guidance), and operational guides (deployment procedures, configuration management, capacity planning).

Each taxonomy category has different authoring and review requirements. Architecture overviews require architecture review board approval before publication. Prompt library entries require security review (for injection risk) and quality review (eval-backed accuracy claims). Eval documentation requires sign-off from the ML quality team. Operational guides require SRE review. Implementing these review requirements as automated checks in the portal (a document in the 'prompt library' category cannot be published without a security-review label in its front matter) ensures that the taxonomy is maintained without relying on manual governance.

Distinguish between internal documentation (only visible to the engineering team) and consumer-facing documentation (visible to internal customers of the agent service, such as product managers, data analysts, or business stakeholders). Internal docs can contain operational details, known limitations, and work-in-progress information. Consumer-facing docs must reflect the current production capability accurately and be written for a non-engineering audience. The portal should support access-controlled publishing with separate internal and consumer-facing views to prevent operational details from leaking to consumers.
Incident-Driven Documentation Improvement

Every incident that is resolved using a runbook is an opportunity to improve that runbook. Make runbook improvement a standard step in the incident post-mortem process. After each incident, the post-mortem template includes a 'documentation impact' section: Was there a runbook for this alert? Did the engineer find it quickly? Were the steps accurate and complete? What additions or corrections would improve the runbook for future incidents? Post-mortem owners submit a PR to the runbook repository with the improvements before the post-mortem is considered closed.

Incidents that were resolved without a runbook—because no runbook existed—require creating a new runbook as a post-mortem action item. A 'no runbook found' label on incidents tracks how often this happens and for which alert types. When the same alert triggers three or more incidents without a runbook, it becomes a P2 priority to create one before the next incident. The IDP portal can automate this tracking: if an alert fires and the alert's runbook_url is missing or 404s, the portal creates an automated GitHub issue in the runbook repository requesting creation of the missing runbook, assigned to the alert's owning team.
Key Takeaways
IDP-integrated documentation reduces incident response time by linking alerts directly to runbooks, eliminating the search step under time pressure.
Freshness tracking and ownership assignment are the minimum viable controls to prevent documentation from becoming an unreliable maintenance burden.
Use stable slug-based URLs for all runbooks and add runbook_url annotations to every alert as a definition-of-done criterion for new alert creation.
Migrate the most-used runbooks (linked to highest-frequency alerts) first; avoid big-bang migration that delays portal value.
Test runbooks in quarterly incident drills using engineers who did not write them to surface gaps before they occur in real incidents.
Additional implementation considerations

Implementing these practices effectively requires understanding both the technical and organizational context in which they will operate. Teams at different stages of AI maturity will apply these practices differently: a team that has operated AI systems in production for two years has a richer base of operational experience to inform their implementation choices than a team deploying their first agent. Neither team should simply adopt a template; both should evaluate each practice against their specific risk profile, operational constraints, and team capabilities.

Cross-functional collaboration is essential for sustainable implementation. Engineering, AI safety, product, and SRE teams all have legitimate interests in how these practices are designed and enforced. Practices designed without input from the teams who must live with them tend to be either too abstract to apply consistently or too prescriptive to accommodate the real variability in situations they will encounter. Invest in the collaborative design process upfront; the time spent building shared understanding saves time in adoption and reduces the resistance that comes from practices imposed without consultation.

Measurement is the foundation of continuous improvement. Each practice described here has an associated metric: review cycle time, DoD completion rate, eval pass rate, incident rate, checklist failure distribution. Teams that track these metrics and review them regularly can identify whether their implementations are achieving their intended effects, where friction is causing workarounds, and where investment in tooling or capacity would provide the highest return. Practices that are not measured are not improved; they either remain static while the environment changes around them or gradually decay as their original purpose is forgotten.

Finally, recognize that the practices described here are means, not ends. The ends are: agent capabilities that work reliably, behave safely, can be operated and maintained by the team, and create genuine value for users. Every practice should be evaluated against these outcomes. When a practice is creating overhead without demonstrably contributing to these outcomes, it should be reformed or replaced. When a practice is clearly preventing incidents or improving quality, it should be maintained even when it creates friction. The discipline of outcome-oriented engineering process management is what separates teams that maintain effective practices long-term from teams whose practices gradually become compliance exercises.
